title: Get Health Insurance (Krankenversicherung)
description: Health insurance is mandatory in Germany from day one of residence. Most new arrivals choose statutory (gesetzlich / GKV) insurance — TK, AOK, BARMER, or DAK. Private insurance (PKV) is only available if your gross salary exceeds the annual threshold (~€69,300 in 2025) or you are self-employed. Students under 30 get subsidised GKV rates of around €120/month.
steps: Determine whether you qualify for statutory (GKV) or private (PKV) insurance based on your employment status and income
Students: contact a major GKV provider (TK, AOK, BARMER) directly — they have English-speaking support lines
Employees: inform HR which GKV insurer you choose — your employer registers you and deducts contributions automatically
Apply online with your passport; once you have your Meldebescheinigung, send a copy to your insurer
Receive your Versicherungskarte (health card) within 2–3 weeks — request a provisional membership letter in the meantime for doctor visits
documents: Meldebescheinigung (after Anmeldung — can apply before, but send it once you have it)
Employment contract or university enrolment letter
communityTips: TK (Techniker Krankenkasse) is the most popular GKV among newcomers — excellent English-language service
Request a provisional membership letter (Vorläufige Mitgliedsbescheinigung) immediately — needed for doctor visits before your health card arrives
You can switch GKV providers after 12 months — your first choice is not permanent

providers.name: TK — Techniker Krankenkasse
providers.note: Most popular GKV for internationals, English support, fast application

providers.name: BARMER
providers.note: Nationwide GKV, solid English service, good app

providers.name: AOK
providers.note: Largest GKV by members, regional offices in every city

title: Get a German SIM Card
description: A German mobile number is required for two-factor authentication on banking apps, online portals, and most government registrations. Prepaid SIMs are available immediately at supermarkets and electronics stores with just a passport — no address needed. A postpaid contract (cheaper long-term) requires a registered address.
steps: Buy a prepaid SIM immediately: Aldi Talk, Lidl Connect, and REWE Mobil are cheapest (from €10, uses Telekom or O2 network)
Activate the SIM online using your passport — mandatory since 2017 under the Telekommunikationsgesetz
Once you have your Meldebescheinigung, compare postpaid tariffs on Check24 or Verivox for a better long-term deal
Switch your WhatsApp, banking apps, and email 2FA to your German number before cancelling any foreign SIM
communityTips: Aldi Talk runs on the Telekom network — the best coverage in both cities and rural areas
Activate your SIM online with your passport — legally required in Germany since 2017
Keep your foreign SIM active temporarily for 2FA while you transition accounts to your German number

providers.name: Aldi Talk
providers.note: Cheapest prepaid, Telekom network, buy at any Aldi store

providers.name: Lidl Connect
providers.note: Budget prepaid, Vodafone network, good urban coverage

providers.name: Blau.de
providers.note: Good postpaid tariffs once you have a registered address

title: Get Biometric Passport Photos
description: Biometric photos (35×45 mm, specific background and lighting requirements) are needed for your residence permit application at the Ausländerbehörde, and sometimes for other registrations. German automated photo booths at train stations (Deutsche Bahn) and post offices produce compliant photos for around €5–8. Chemist studios like dm Fotowelt also offer this service.
steps: Find a photo booth at your nearest train station, post office, or dm/Rossmann drugstore
Select the 'Biometrisches Passfoto' option — the machine will guide you on posture and expression
Get at least 4 copies printed — you will need them for the Ausländerbehörde and potentially for other documents
Check the Bundesdruckerei photo guidelines if you are unsure whether a photo meets requirements
timeEstimate: 15 min at a train station or drugstore
communityTips: Get at least 4 copies printed — you'll use them for the Ausländerbehörde, residence permit, and other forms
Photo booths at Deutsche Bahn stations (look for 'Biometrisches Passfoto') are the most reliable for compliance
dm and Rossmann drugstores also have photo studios — slightly cheaper than train station booths

title: Anmeldung (City Registration)
description: Registering your address at the local Bürgeramt is legally required within 14 days of moving in. The Meldebescheinigung (registration certificate) you receive is the master document — you need it to open a bank account, receive your tax ID, apply for your residence permit, and complete almost every other administrative step.
steps: Find your local Bürgeramt on your city's official website
Book an appointment online — walk-in slots are rare and often unavailable; book as early as possible
Collect your documents: passport, completed Anmeldeformular, and the Wohnungsgeberbestätigung (landlord confirmation form) signed by your landlord
Attend your appointment and receive your Meldebescheinigung on the spot
Store several copies of the Meldebescheinigung — you will need it repeatedly in the coming weeks
documents: Valid passport or national ID card
Completed Anmeldeformular (registration form — download from your city's Bürgeramt website)
Wohnungsgeberbestätigung (landlord confirmation form — must be signed by your landlord)
communityTips: Book your appointment the day you arrive — in Berlin, slots fill 4–8 weeks ahead
The Wohnungsgeberbestätigung must be signed by your landlord — many landlords don't know this form; bring a printed template
You'll receive your Meldebescheinigung on the same day — make at least 5 photocopies immediately
Some Bürgeramt offices accept walk-ins early morning — worth trying if online slots are fully booked

title: Complete University Enrolment & Get Student Card
description: Full enrolment (Immatrikulation) is separate from your admission offer — you must submit documents, pay the semester fee (Semesterbeitrag), and collect your physical student card (Studierendenausweis). The student card is often also your public transport pass for the semester, gives you access to campus facilities, and proves student status for discounts and health insurance.
steps: Log in to your university's student portal and complete the enrolment checklist
Pay the semester fee (typically €100–400 depending on the university and state) — this often includes a public transport semester ticket
Upload or submit required documents: passport, visa, proof of health insurance, university admission letter
Collect your physical Studierendenausweis from the student office (Studierendensekretariat) or activate the digital version
Register with your faculty/department and enrol in courses for the semester

title: Register Your Electricity & Gas Account
description: In Germany, the person living in the flat is responsible for the electricity and gas contract, unless your landlord includes utilities in the rent (check your lease). When you move in, note the meter readings (Zählerstand) and report them to the basic supplier (Grundversorger) for your area. You can then switch to a cheaper provider using a comparison portal.
steps: Check your rental contract — if utilities (Nebenkosten) are included, you do not need a separate contract
Note the electricity (Strom) and gas (Gas) meter readings on your move-in day and photograph them
Contact the local Grundversorger (default supplier) to register as the new tenant — they are legally required to supply you
Compare tariffs on Check24, Verivox, or Stromauskunft.de and switch to a cheaper provider if desired
Set up a SEPA direct debit (Lastschrift) with your German bank account for monthly advance payments
communityTips: Check your lease carefully — if utilities (Nebenkosten) are included you skip this entirely
Photograph your meter readings on move-in day — protects you from disputes about prior tenants' usage
Switching to Check24 or Verivox after initial registration with the Grundversorger can save €200–400/year

title: Get the Deutschlandticket (€58/month)
description: The Deutschlandticket (formerly €49-Ticket) gives unlimited travel on all regional and local public transport across Germany for €58/month. It covers U-Bahn, S-Bahn, buses, trams, and regional trains (not ICE/IC). It requires a German bank account for the monthly direct debit. Students may have a subsidised version included in their semester fee — check before buying.
steps: Students: check whether your semester fee already includes a public transport pass (Semesterticket) — if so, you do not need the Deutschlandticket
Purchase through your local transport authority app (BVG in Berlin, MVV in Munich, RMV in Frankfurt, HVV in Hamburg, KVB in Cologne) or directly through DB Navigator
A German IBAN is required for the monthly SEPA direct debit — set up your bank account first
The ticket auto-renews monthly; cancel by the 10th of the month to stop next month's charge
Download the app and save the ticket digitally — it is not available as a physical card
communityTips: Students: check if your semester fee already includes a transport pass (Semesterticket) — you may not need the Deutschlandticket
Cancel by the 10th of the month to stop the next month's payment — subscriptions auto-renew otherwise
Works on all regional trains (not ICE/IC) — makes weekend trips across Germany very affordable

title: Open a German Bank Account
description: A German bank account (Girokonto) is essential for receiving salary, paying rent by SEPA transfer, and setting up direct debits. Most landlords and employers require a German IBAN. You will need your Meldebescheinigung and passport to open one. Online banks are faster for new arrivals as they do not require in-person visits.
steps: Complete your Anmeldung first — most banks require a Meldebescheinigung
Choose between a traditional bank (Deutsche Bank, Sparkasse, Commerzbank) or a fully online bank (N26, DKB, Revolut DE) — online banks are often faster and English-friendly
Apply online or in-branch with your passport and Meldebescheinigung
Complete identity verification (online: VideoIdent via app; in-branch: in person with passport)
Wait 5–10 business days to receive your debit card and PIN by separate post

officialLinks.label: BaFin — Consumer information on payment accounts

timeEstimate: 15 min to apply, 5–10 days to receive card
documents: German phone number (for 2FA during account setup)
communityTips: N26 and DKB are the most popular choices for newcomers — English apps, fast setup, no monthly fees
DKB requires a German address; N26 can sometimes be opened without a Meldebescheinigung
Order your card immediately after applying — landlords and employers will need your IBAN quickly

providers.name: N26
providers.note: Most popular for newcomers, English app, free international transfers

providers.name: DKB (Deutsche Kreditbank)
providers.note: No monthly fee, free VISA debit card, great for students

providers.name: Commerzbank
providers.note: Traditional bank, English support, branch access nationwide

title: Get Personal Liability Insurance (Haftpflichtversicherung)
description: Personal liability insurance is not legally mandatory in Germany but is considered essential. It covers you if you accidentally damage someone else's property or injure someone — for example, breaking a landlord's window, causing a cycling accident, or a friend tripping over something in your flat. Policies typically cost €3–6 per month and can be cancelled monthly.
steps: Compare policies on Check24.de or Verivox.de — filter for 'Privathaftpflichtversicherung'
Look for a policy with at least €10 million coverage (€10 Mio. Deckungssumme) — this is standard
Check whether your policy covers: key loss (Schlüsselverlust), rented property damage (Mietsachschäden), and bike accidents
Apply online — most providers accept a German bank account for SEPA direct debit
Keep your policy document (Versicherungsschein) digitally and share your insurer's emergency number with close contacts
communityTips: Getsafe and Feather offer English-language policies specifically designed for expats in Germany
Always add Mietsachschäden (rented property damage) cover — not included in all standard policies
Most policies allow monthly cancellation — easy to switch if you find a better deal later

providers.name: Feather Insurance
providers.note: English, designed for internationals, monthly cancellable

title: Set Up Home Internet (DSL / Cable)
description: Home broadband in Germany takes 2–4 weeks to activate after signing a contract, so order early. Most contracts run for 24 months with a 1-month notice period. In shared flats (WGs), check whether internet is already included in the rent before ordering a new line. The main providers are Telekom, Vodafone, O2, and 1&1.
steps: Check your rental contract — internet may already be included in the Nebenkosten
In a WG: ask flatmates whether there is an existing contract you can split costs for
Compare offers on Check24.de using your postcode to see which providers service your building
Order online — you will need your registered address (Meldebescheinigung) and German IBAN
A technician appointment (Technikertermin) is usually required; book the earliest available slot as waiting times can be 2–6 weeks

title: Register for Broadcasting Fee (Rundfunkbeitrag)
description: Every household in Germany must pay the Rundfunkbeitrag (public broadcasting fee) of €18.36/month. It is per household, not per person — if you share a flat where someone is already paying, you are exempt. Students with BAföG or Wohngeld can apply for a full exemption. Register online to avoid retroactive charges.
steps: Check if a flatmate in your household is already registered — if so, you do not need to register separately
If you are the sole or first resident, go to rundfunkbeitrag.de and click 'Anmelden'
Fill in your name and registered address (must match your Meldebescheinigung)
Choose a payment method — quarterly direct debit (SEPA) is the default
Students receiving BAföG: apply for exemption (Befreiung) on the same website with your BAföG notice

officialLinks.label: Rundfunkbeitrag — Befreiung (exemption for students / low income)

title: Register with a GP (Hausarzt)
description: Having a registered GP (Hausarzt) means you can get same-day or next-day appointments rather than waiting weeks as a new patient. You do not need to formally register in advance — simply call a practice and ask to become a patient (Patient werden). Present your Versicherungskarte (health insurance card) at your first appointment.
steps: Search for GPs accepting new patients on the KBV Arztsuche or your health insurer's app — filter by 'Hausarzt' and your postcode
Call practices directly and ask 'Nehmen Sie neue Patienten an?' (Are you accepting new patients?)
Book a first appointment (Erstgespräch) — bring your passport, Meldebescheinigung, and health insurance card
Tell the GP you are new to Germany so they can set up a full patient record
Ask for referrals (Überweisungen) to specialists from your GP — going directly to a specialist without referral often costs extra

title: Household Contents Insurance (Hausratversicherung)
description: Household contents insurance covers your personal belongings — furniture, electronics, clothing — against theft, fire, water damage, and storms. It is not legally required but strongly recommended, especially if you own expensive items. The premium is based on the size of your flat, typically €50–100/year for a 50 m² apartment.
steps: Estimate the total replacement value of your belongings — this determines the coverage sum (Versicherungssumme)
Compare policies on Check24.de or Verivox.de — look for 'Hausratversicherung'
Check whether your policy covers: bicycle theft (Fahrraddiebstahl), glass breakage (Glasbruch), and items temporarily taken outside the home
Apply online with your address and flat size — payment via German IBAN is required
Store your policy number and insurer's claims hotline — you must report theft to the police (Polizeibericht) before filing a claim

title: Register for an Integration Course (Integrationskurs)
description: Integration courses are subsidised by the German government and combine German language training (up to B1 level, 600 hours) with an orientation course on German law, history, and culture (100 hours). They are free or heavily subsidised for eligible newcomers. Family reunion visa holders are often legally required to attend; other visa types can attend voluntarily.
steps: Check whether you are eligible and required to attend via the BAMF website
Apply for course admission at the BAMF or through an authorised course provider — you will need your passport, visa, and Meldebescheinigung
Find a local course provider using the BAMF course search tool — courses are offered by VHS, private language schools, and churches
Pay the reduced participant contribution (currently €1.95/hour after subsidies; free if receiving Bürgergeld or BAföG)
Attend all sessions — missing more than 10% of hours may disqualify you from receiving the completion certificate (Integrationskurszeugnis)

officialLinks.label: BAMF — Find a course near you

title: Apply for Residence Permit (Aufenthaltstitel)
description: Non-EU nationals must apply for a German residence permit (Aufenthaltstitel) at the local Ausländerbehörde (immigration office). Your entry visa is temporary — the residence permit is what allows you to legally stay, work, and study long-term. Book an appointment as early as possible: waiting times in major cities can be 6–12 weeks.
steps: Book an appointment at your local Ausländerbehörde as soon as you arrive — do not wait until your visa is about to expire

Prepare your documents: valid passport, current visa, Meldebescheinigung, biometric passport photos (35×45 mm), proof of health insurance, proof of income or financing (employment contract, blocked account statement, or scholarship letter), and enrolment certificate if studying
Complete and print the application form for the relevant permit type from your city's Ausländerbehörde website
Attend your appointment and pay the processing fee (€100–110 for most permit types)
You will receive a temporary residence permit (Fiktionsbescheinigung) on the day; the full card arrives by post within 4–6 weeks
documents: Valid passport with current entry visa
Proof of health insurance (membership letter from insurer)
Proof of income or financing (employment contract, scholarship letter, or blocked account statement)
Completed application form (download from your city's Ausländerbehörde website)
communityTips: Book your appointment the moment you arrive — waiting times in Berlin and Munich can reach 10–12 weeks
The Fiktionsbescheinigung you receive on the day legally allows you to stay and work while your card is processed
Bring more documents than you think you need — officers can reject applications for missing items and re-booking takes weeks

title: Opt Out of Church Tax (Kirchenaustritt)
description: If you are registered in Germany as a member of a Catholic, Protestant, or Jewish religious community (indicated in your home country records), you will automatically be charged Kirchensteuer (church tax) — 8–9% of your income tax bill, roughly 0.4–0.8% of gross salary. To stop paying, you must formally declare your exit (Kirchenaustritt) at the local registry office or court. This has no effect on your faith — it is purely administrative.
steps: Confirm whether you are subject to church tax by checking your first German payslip (look for 'KiSt' deductions) or asking your employer
Locate your local Amtsgericht (district court) or Standesamt (registry office) — the correct office depends on your federal state
Book an appointment — in some states (e.g., Bayern), you can declare by post or online
Bring your passport and Meldebescheinigung; pay the small processing fee (€25–35 depending on state)
You will receive a written confirmation; send a copy to your employer's payroll department to stop future deductions

officialLinks.label: BMJ — Legal basis for church tax in Germany

title: Enrol in a German Language Course (VHS)
description: Even basic German significantly reduces bureaucratic stress and helps with daily life. Volkshochschulen (VHS) — public adult education centres — offer courses from A1 to C1 at heavily subsidised rates (€80–200 per semester). Courses are available in the evenings and weekends. Private providers like Goethe-Institut offer more intensive options.
steps: Find your nearest Volkshochschule using the VHS course search and browse their German course calendar
Assess your current level — most VHS providers offer free placement tests online or in person
Register online or in person for the next semester — courses fill quickly, so register early
Bring your Meldebescheinigung for registration; some VHS offer discounts for Bürgergeld or BAföG recipients
Supplement classes with free apps (Deutsche Welle's 'Deutsch Warum Nicht', Duolingo, Babbel) for daily practice

officialLinks.label: Volkshochschule — Find a VHS near you

title: Convert Your Driver's Licence
description: If you hold a non-EU driving licence, it is generally valid in Germany for 6 months from the date of registration (Anmeldung). After that, you must convert it to a German licence (Führerschein) — the process varies significantly by country of issue. Some licences (e.g., from Japan, South Korea, USA certain states) can be converted without a test; others require a theory and/or practical exam.
steps: Check whether your country of issue has a bilateral agreement with Germany on the BMDV website — this determines whether you need tests
Get your foreign licence officially translated by a certified translator (vereidigter Übersetzer) or ADAC — not all offices require this, but it speeds up the process

Apply at your local Führerscheinstelle (driving licence office) with: passport, Meldebescheinigung, current foreign licence, translation, biometric photo, and a first aid certificate (Erste-Hilfe-Kurs, valid if taken recently)
If required, pass the theory exam (Theorieprüfung) at a TÜV or DEKRA centre — an official German driving school can help you prepare
Pay the conversion fee (€35–60) and wait 2–4 weeks for the German Führerschein to arrive

title: Tax ID (Steueridentifikationsnummer)
description: Your Steueridentifikationsnummer (Steuer-ID) is an 11-digit number assigned automatically by the Bundeszentralamt für Steuern after your Anmeldung is processed. It is sent by post to your registered address within 2–4 weeks and is required by your employer for payroll. You keep this number for life, even if you leave and return to Germany.
steps: Complete your Anmeldung — your Steuer-ID is issued automatically afterwards, no separate application needed
Wait 2–4 weeks for the letter from the Bundeszentralamt für Steuern (BZSt) to arrive at your registered address
If your employer needs it urgently before the letter arrives, request it online via the BZSt website using your name, address, and date of birth
Provide the 11-digit number to your employer's HR or payroll team

title: Get Your Social Insurance Number (Sozialversicherungsnummer)
description: Your Sozialversicherungsnummer (SVNR) is a 12-digit number issued by the Deutsche Rentenversicherung (DRV) and is required before you can start any paid employment in Germany. For employees, it is usually applied for automatically by the first employer. Students working part-time (Minijob or Werkstudent) also need it. The SVNR stays with you for life.
steps: For employed workers: inform your employer before your start date — they will register you with the relevant health insurer, which then automatically notifies the DRV
The DRV will send your Sozialversicherungsausweis (insurance card) with your SVNR by post to your registered address within 2–4 weeks
If you need the SVNR urgently, contact your health insurer — they can look up your existing number if you had one from a previous stay in Germany
Students with a Minijob: your employer registers you with the Minijobzentrale, which issues your SVNR
Store the physical Sozialversicherungsausweis card safely — replace a lost card by applying to the DRV online

title: Register on ELSTER (Tax Return Portal)
description: ELSTER (Elektronische Steuererklärung) is the official German online portal for submitting tax returns. Registering takes 2–3 weeks because the activation certificate arrives by post. File your first German tax return (Steuererklärung) for the year you arrive — employed workers on a standard tax class often receive a refund of €500–1,500 due to partial-year employment.
steps: Go to elster.de and click 'Benutzerkonto erstellen' — choose 'Privatperson' as your account type
Enter your Steuer-ID, date of birth, and registered address; submit the online form
Wait 1–2 weeks for an activation letter to arrive at your registered address containing a code
Complete the online activation using the code and set a secure password
Use ELSTER or a compatible app (Taxfix, Wundertax, SteuerGo) to file your tax return for the current year by 31 July of the following year
